90	    def date_extract_sql(self, lookup_type, sql, params):
91	        extract_sql = f"TO_CHAR({sql}, %s)"
92	        extract_param = None
93	        if lookup_type == "week_day":
94	            # TO_CHAR(field, 'D') returns an integer from 1-7, where 1=Sunday.
95	            extract_param = "D"
96	        elif lookup_type == "iso_week_day":
97	            extract_sql = f"TO_CHAR({sql} - 1, %s)"
98	            extract_param = "D"
99	        elif lookup_type == "week":
100	            # IW = ISO week number
101	            extract_param = "IW"
102	        elif lookup_type == "quarter":
103	            extract_param = "Q"
104	        elif lookup_type == "iso_year":
105	            extract_param = "IYYY"
106	        else:
107	            lookup_type = lookup_type.upper()
108	            if not self._extract_format_re.fullmatch(lookup_type):
109	                raise ValueError(f"Invalid loookup type: {lookup_type!r}")
110	            # https://docs.oracle.com/en/database/oracle/oracle-database/21/sqlrf/EXTRACT-datetime.html
111	            return f"EXTRACT({lookup_type} FROM {sql})", params
112	        return extract_sql, (*params, extract_param)

114	    def date_trunc_sql(self, lookup_type, sql, params, tzname=None):
115	        sql, params = self._convert_sql_to_tz(sql, params, tzname)
116	        # https://docs.oracle.com/en/database/oracle/oracle-database/21/sqlrf/ROUND-and-TRUNC-Date-Functions.html
117	        trunc_param = None
118	        if lookup_type in ("year", "month"):
119	            trunc_param = lookup_type.upper()
120	        elif lookup_type == "quarter":
121	            trunc_param = "Q"
122	        elif lookup_type == "week":
123	            trunc_param = "IW"
124	        else:
125	            return f"TRUNC({sql})", params
126	        return f"TRUNC({sql}, %s)", (*params, trunc_param)

134	    def _prepare_tzname_delta(self, tzname):
135	        tzname, sign, offset = split_tzname_delta(tzname)
136	        return f"{sign}{offset}" if offset else tzname

138	    def _convert_sql_to_tz(self, sql, params, tzname):
139	        if not (settings.USE_TZ and tzname):
140	            return sql, params
141	        if not self._tzname_re.match(tzname):
142	            raise ValueError("Invalid time zone name: %s" % tzname)
143	        # Convert from connection timezone to the local time, returning
144	        # TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE and cast it back to TIMESTAMP to strip the
145	        # TIME ZONE details.
146	        if self.connection.timezone_name != tzname:
147	            from_timezone_name = self.connection.timezone_name
148	            to_timezone_name = self._prepare_tzname_delta(tzname)
149	            return (
150	                f"CAST((FROM_TZ({sql}, '{from_timezone_name}') AT TIME ZONE "
151	                f"'{to_timezone_name}') AS TIMESTAMP)",
152	                params,
153	            )
154	        return sql, params

160	    def datetime_cast_time_sql(self, sql, params, tzname):
161	        # Since `TimeField` values are stored as TIMESTAMP change to the
162	        # default date and convert the field to the specified timezone.
163	        sql, params = self._convert_sql_to_tz(sql, params, tzname)
164	        convert_datetime_sql = (
165	            f"TO_TIMESTAMP(CONCAT('1900-01-01 ', TO_CHAR({sql}, 'HH24:MI:SS.FF')), "
166	            f"'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS.FF')"
167	        )
168	        return (
169	            f"CASE WHEN {sql} IS NOT NULL THEN {convert_datetime_sql} ELSE NULL END",
170	            (*params, *params),
171	        )

174	        sql, params = self._convert_sql_to_tz(sql, params, tzname)
175	        if lookup_type == "second":

177	            return f"FLOOR(EXTRACT(SECOND FROM {sql}))", params

180	    def datetime_trunc_sql(self, lookup_type, sql, params, tzname):
181	        sql, params = self._convert_sql_to_tz(sql, params, tzname)
182	        # https://docs.oracle.com/en/database/oracle/oracle-database/21/sqlrf/ROUND-and-TRUNC-Date-Functions.html
183	        trunc_param = None
184	        if lookup_type in ("year", "month"):
185	            trunc_param = lookup_type.upper()
186	        elif lookup_type == "quarter":
187	            trunc_param = "Q"
188	        elif lookup_type == "week":
189	            trunc_param = "IW"
190	        elif lookup_type == "hour":
191	            trunc_param = "HH24"
192	        elif lookup_type == "minute":
193	            trunc_param = "MI"

202	        if lookup_type == "second":

204	            return f"FLOOR(EXTRACT(SECOND FROM {sql}))", params

207	    def time_trunc_sql(self, lookup_type, sql, params, tzname=None):
208	        # The implementation is similar to `datetime_trunc_sql` as both
209	        # `DateTimeField` and `TimeField` are stored as TIMESTAMP where
210	        # the date part of the later is ignored.
211	        sql, params = self._convert_sql_to_tz(sql, params, tzname)
212	        trunc_param = None
213	        if lookup_type == "hour":

217	        elif lookup_type == "second":

222	    def get_db_converters(self, expression):
223	        converters = super().get_db_converters(expression)
224	        internal_type = expression.output_field.get_internal_type()
225	        if internal_type in ["JSONField", "TextField"]:
226	            converters.append(self.convert_textfield_value)
227	        elif internal_type == "BinaryField":
228	            converters.append(self.convert_binaryfield_value)
229	        elif internal_type == "BooleanField":
230	            converters.append(self.convert_booleanfield_value)
231	        elif internal_type == "DateTimeField":
232	            if settings.USE_TZ:
233	                converters.append(self.convert_datetimefield_value)
234	        elif internal_type == "DateField":
235	            converters.append(self.convert_datefield_value)
236	        elif internal_type == "TimeField":
237	            converters.append(self.convert_timefield_value)
238	        elif internal_type == "UUIDField":
239	            converters.append(self.convert_uuidfield_value)
240	        # Oracle stores empty strings as null. If the field accepts the empty
241	        # string, undo this to adhere to the Django convention of using
242	        # the empty string instead of null.
243	        if expression.output_field.empty_strings_allowed:
244	            converters.append(
245	                self.convert_empty_bytes
246	                if internal_type == "BinaryField"
247	                else self.convert_empty_string
248	            )
249	        return converters

337	    def last_executed_query(self, cursor, sql, params):
338	        # https://python-oracledb.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api_manual/cursor.html#Cursor.statement
339	        # The DB API definition does not define this attribute.
340	        statement = cursor.statement
341	        # Unlike Psycopg's `query` and MySQLdb`'s `_executed`, oracledb's
342	        # `statement` doesn't contain the query parameters. Substitute
343	        # parameters manually.
344	        if statement and params:
345	            if isinstance(params, (tuple, list)):
346	                params = {
347	                    f":arg{i}": param for i, param in enumerate(dict.fromkeys(params))
348	                }
349	            elif isinstance(params, dict):
350	                params = {f":{key}": val for (key, val) in params.items()}
351	            for key in sorted(params, key=len, reverse=True):
352	                statement = statement.replace(
353	                    key, force_str(params[key], errors="replace")
354	                )
355	        return statement

389	    def quote_name(self, name):
390	        # SQL92 requires delimited (quoted) names to be case-sensitive. When
391	        # not quoted, Oracle has case-insensitive behavior for identifiers, but
392	        # always defaults to uppercase.
393	        # We simplify things by making Oracle identifiers always uppercase.
394	        if not name.startswith('"') and not name.endswith('"'):
395	            name = '"%s"' % truncate_name(name, self.max_name_length())
396	        # Oracle puts the query text into a (query % args) construct, so %
397	        # signs in names need to be escaped. The '%%' will be collapsed back to
398	        # '%' at that stage so we aren't really making the name longer here.
399	        name = name.replace("%", "%%")
400	        return name.upper()

409	    def __foreign_key_constraints(self, table_name, recursive):
410	        with self.connection.cursor() as cursor:
411	            if recursive:
412	                cursor.execute(
413	                    """
414	                    SELECT
415	                        user_tables.table_name, rcons.constraint_name
416	                    FROM
417	                        user_tables
418	                    JOIN
419	                        user_constraints cons
420	                        ON (user_tables.table_name = cons.table_name
421	                        AND cons.constraint_type = ANY('P', 'U'))
422	                    LEFT JOIN
423	                        user_constraints rcons
424	                        ON (user_tables.table_name = rcons.table_name
425	                        AND rcons.constraint_type = 'R')
426	                    START WITH user_tables.table_name = UPPER(%s)
427	                    CONNECT BY
428	                        NOCYCLE PRIOR cons.constraint_name = rcons.r_constraint_name
429	                    GROUP BY
430	                        user_tables.table_name, rcons.constraint_name
431	                    HAVING user_tables.table_name != UPPER(%s)
432	                    ORDER BY MAX(level) DESC
433	                    """,
434	                    (table_name, table_name),
435	                )
436	            else:
437	                cursor.execute(
438	                    """
439	                    SELECT
440	                        cons.table_name, cons.constraint_name
441	                    FROM
442	                        user_constraints cons
443	                    WHERE
444	                        cons.constraint_type = 'R'
445	                        AND cons.table_name = UPPER(%s)
446	                    """,
447	                    (table_name,),
448	                )
449	            return cursor.fetchall()

457	    def sql_flush(self, style, tables, *, reset_sequences=False, allow_cascade=False):
458	        if not tables:
459	            return []
460	
461	        truncated_tables = {table.upper() for table in tables}
462	        constraints = set()
463	        # Oracle's TRUNCATE CASCADE only works with ON DELETE CASCADE foreign
464	        # keys which Django doesn't define. Emulate the PostgreSQL behavior
465	        # which truncates all dependent tables by manually retrieving all
466	        # foreign key constraints and resolving dependencies.
467	        for table in tables:
468	            for foreign_table, constraint in self._foreign_key_constraints(
469	                table, recursive=allow_cascade
470	            ):
471	                if allow_cascade:
472	                    truncated_tables.add(foreign_table)
473	                constraints.add((foreign_table, constraint))
474	        sql = (
475	            [
476	                "%s %s %s %s %s %s %s %s;"
477	                % (
478	                    style.SQL_KEYWORD("ALTER"),
479	                    style.SQL_KEYWORD("TABLE"),
480	                    style.SQL_FIELD(self.quote_name(table)),
481	                    style.SQL_KEYWORD("DISABLE"),
482	                    style.SQL_KEYWORD("CONSTRAINT"),
483	                    style.SQL_FIELD(self.quote_name(constraint)),
484	                    style.SQL_KEYWORD("KEEP"),
485	                    style.SQL_KEYWORD("INDEX"),
486	                )
487	                for table, constraint in constraints
488	            ]
489	            + [
490	                "%s %s %s;"
491	                % (
492	                    style.SQL_KEYWORD("TRUNCATE"),
493	                    style.SQL_KEYWORD("TABLE"),
494	                    style.SQL_FIELD(self.quote_name(table)),
495	                )
496	                for table in truncated_tables
497	            ]
498	            + [
499	                "%s %s %s %s %s %s;"
500	                % (
501	                    style.SQL_KEYWORD("ALTER"),
502	                    style.SQL_KEYWORD("TABLE"),
503	                    style.SQL_FIELD(self.quote_name(table)),
504	                    style.SQL_KEYWORD("ENABLE"),
505	                    style.SQL_KEYWORD("CONSTRAINT"),
506	                    style.SQL_FIELD(self.quote_name(constraint)),
507	                )
508	                for table, constraint in constraints
509	            ]
510	        )
511	        if reset_sequences:
512	            sequences = [
513	                sequence
514	                for sequence in self.connection.introspection.sequence_list()
515	                if sequence["table"].upper() in truncated_tables
516	            ]
517	            # Since we've just deleted all the rows, running our sequence ALTER
518	            # code will reset the sequence to 0.
519	            sql.extend(self.sequence_reset_by_name_sql(style, sequences))
520	        return sql

522	    def sequence_reset_by_name_sql(self, style, sequences):
523	        sql = []
524	        for sequence_info in sequences:
525	            no_autofield_sequence_name = self._get_no_autofield_sequence_name(
526	                sequence_info["table"]
527	            )
528	            table = self.quote_name(sequence_info["table"])
529	            column = self.quote_name(sequence_info["column"] or "id")
530	            query = self._sequence_reset_sql % {
531	                "no_autofield_sequence_name": no_autofield_sequence_name,
532	                "table": table,
533	                "column": column,
534	                "table_name": strip_quotes(table),
535	                "column_name": strip_quotes(column),
536	                "suffix": self.connection.features.bare_select_suffix,
537	            }
538	            sql.append(query)
539	        return sql

586	    def adapt_datetimefield_value(self, value):
587	        """
588	        Transform a datetime value to an object compatible with what is
589	        expected by the backend driver for datetime columns.
590	
591	        If naive datetime is passed assumes that is in UTC. Normally Django
592	        models.DateTimeField makes sure that if USE_TZ is True passed datetime
593	        is timezone aware.
594	        """
595	
596	        if value is None:
597	            return None
598	
599	        # oracledb doesn't support tz-aware datetimes
600	        if timezone.is_aware(value):
601	            if settings.USE_TZ:
602	                value = timezone.make_naive(value, self.connection.timezone)
603	            else:
604	                raise ValueError(
605	                    "Oracle backend does not support timezone-aware datetimes when "
606	                    "USE_TZ is False."
607	                )
608	
609	        return Oracle_datetime.from_datetime(value)

611	    def adapt_timefield_value(self, value):
612	        if value is None:
613	            return None
614	
615	        if isinstance(value, str):
616	            return datetime.datetime.strptime(value, "%H:%M:%S")
617	
618	        # Oracle doesn't support tz-aware times
619	        if timezone.is_aware(value):
620	            raise ValueError("Oracle backend does not support timezone-aware times.")
621	
622	        return Oracle_datetime(
623	            1900, 1, 1, value.hour, value.minute, value.second, value.microsecond
624	        )

626	    def combine_expression(self, connector, sub_expressions):
627	        lhs, rhs = sub_expressions
628	        if connector == "%%":
629	            return "MOD(%s)" % ",".join(sub_expressions)
630	        elif connector == "&":
631	            return "BITAND(%s)" % ",".join(sub_expressions)
632	        elif connector == "|":
633	            return "BITAND(-%(lhs)s-1,%(rhs)s)+%(lhs)s" % {"lhs": lhs, "rhs": rhs}
634	        elif connector == "<<":
635	            return "(%(lhs)s * POWER(2, %(rhs)s))" % {"lhs": lhs, "rhs": rhs}
636	        elif connector == ">>":
637	            return "FLOOR(%(lhs)s / POWER(2, %(rhs)s))" % {"lhs": lhs, "rhs": rhs}
638	        elif connector == "^":
639	            return "POWER(%s)" % ",".join(sub_expressions)
640	        elif connector == "#":
641	            raise NotSupportedError("Bitwise XOR is not supported in Oracle.")
642	        return super().combine_expression(connector, sub_expressions)

## Summary of Timezone-Related Code in Django Database Backends

Based on my analysis of the Django database backend codebase, here are the findings:

### 1. MySQL Backend (`/Users/lysaniuk/miniconda3/lib/python3.13/site-packages/django/db/backends/mysql/operations.py`)

**Timezone Conversion Method:**
- **`_convert_sql_to_tz()`** (lines 92-99): Converts SQL expressions to target timezone using MySQL's `CONVERT_TZ()` function
  - Uses `CONVERT_TZ(sql, from_tz, to_tz)` when timezone conversion is needed
  - Calls `_prepare_tzname_delta()` to handle timezone names with offsets
  - Only converts if `settings.USE_TZ` is True and timezones differ

- **`datetime_cast_date_sql()`** (lines 101-103): Casts datetime to date
  - Applies timezone conversion first: `sql, params = self._convert_sql_to_tz(sql, params, tzname)`
  - Returns `DATE(converted_sql)`

- **`datetime_cast_time_sql()`** (lines 105-107): Casts datetime to time
  - Applies timezone conversion first
  - Returns `TIME(converted_sql)`

- **`datetime_extract_sql()`** (lines 109-111): Extracts date parts from datetime
  - Applies timezone conversion, then delegates to `date_extract_sql()`

- **`datetime_trunc_sql()`** (lines 113-136): Truncates datetime to specified precision
  - Applies timezone conversion first

**Hardcoded UTC Reference:**
- Line 410 in `/Users/lysaniuk/miniconda3/lib/python3.13/site-packages/django/db/backends/mysql/base.py`: Test query uses `CONVERT_TZ('2001-01-01 01:00:00', 'UTC', 'UTC')` to verify timezone database is loaded

### 2. SQLite3 Backend (`/Users/lysaniuk/miniconda3/lib/python3.13/site-packages/django/db/backends/sqlite3/operations.py`)

**Timezone Conversion Method:**
- **`_convert_tznames_to_sql()`** (lines 105-108): Helper that returns timezone names for SQL functions
  - Returns `(tzname, connection.timezone_name)` when `settings.USE_TZ` is True
  - Returns `(None, None)` otherwise

- **`datetime_cast_date_sql()`** (lines 110-114): Delegates to custom SQLite user function
  - Calls `django_datetime_cast_date(sql, tzname, connection_tz)`
  - Timezone parameters passed to user-defined function

- **`datetime_cast_time_sql()`** (lines 116-120): Delegates to custom SQLite user function
  - Calls `django_datetime_cast_time(sql, tzname, connection_tz)`

- **`datetime_extract_sql()`** (lines 122-127): Delegates to custom SQLite function
  - Calls `django_datetime_extract(lookup_type, sql, tzname, connection_tz)`

- **`datetime_trunc_sql()`** (lines 129-134): Delegates to custom SQLite function
  - Calls `django_datetime_trunc(lookup_type, sql, tzname, connection_tz)`

**Note:** SQLite3 doesn't support native timezone functions, so Django uses custom Python functions registered with SQLite.

### 3. Oracle Backend (`/Users/lysaniuk/miniconda3/lib/python3.13/site-packages/django/db/backends/oracle/operations.py`)

**Timezone Conversion Method:**
- **`_convert_sql_to_tz()`** (lines 138-154): Converts SQL using Oracle's `FROM_TZ()` and `AT TIME ZONE` syntax
  - Uses `FROM_TZ(sql, 'from_timezone') AT TIME ZONE 'to_timezone'`
  - Casts result to TIMESTAMP to strip timezone details
  - Only converts if `settings.USE_TZ` is True and timezones differ
  - Validates timezone names with regex pattern `_tzname_re` (line 132)

- **`datetime_cast_date_sql()`** (lines 156-158): Casts datetime to date
  - Applies timezone conversion first

- **`datetime_cast_time_sql()`** (lines 160-171): Casts datetime to time
  - Applies timezone conversion first
  - Complex logic: converts to TIMESTAMP with time part, handles NULL values
  - Uses `TO_TIMESTAMP()` and `TO_CHAR()` for formatting

- **`datetime_extract_sql()`** (lines 173-178): Extracts date parts
  - Applies timezone conversion first
  - Special handling for seconds (truncates fractional part)

- **`datetime_trunc_sql()`** (lines 180-199): Truncates datetime to specified precision
  - Applies timezone conversion first

**Hardcoded UTC Reference:**

### 4. PostgreSQL Backend (for reference) (`/Users/lysaniuk/miniconda3/lib/python3.13/site-packages/django/db/backends/postgresql/operations.py`)

**Timezone Conversion Method:**

| Feature | MySQL | SQLite3 | Oracle | PostgreSQL |
|---------|-------|---------|--------|-----------|
| **Timezone Function** | `CONVERT_TZ()` | User-defined functions | `FROM_TZ() AT TIME ZONE` | `AT TIME ZONE` |
| **Connection Timezone** | `connection.timezone_name` | `connection.timezone_name` | `connection.timezone_name` | (via session) |
| **Hardcoded UTC** | Yes (base.py:410) | No | Yes (base.py:320) | No |
| **Timezone Validation** | No regex | No validation | Regex validation | No regex |

All backends follow the same pattern: check `settings.USE_TZ`, compare connection timezone with target timezone, and apply conversion only if they differ.
